title: What is the difference between JDK, JRE, and JVM?
options: JVM executes bytecode; JRE = JVM + core libraries (runs Java apps); JDK = JRE + compiler + tools (develops Java apps). JDK is for developers, JRE for end users
correct_answer: JVM executes bytecode; JRE = JVM + core libraries (runs Java apps); JDK = JRE + compiler + tools (develops Java apps). JDK is for developers, JRE for end users

title: What is the difference between == and .equals() in Java?
options: == compares references (memory addresses) for objects, values for primitives; .equals() compares logical equality (content). Always use .equals() for String, Integer, etc. comparisons
They are identical for all types
correct_answer: == compares references (memory addresses) for objects, values for primitives; .equals() compares logical equality (content). Always use .equals() for String, Integer, etc. comparisons

title: What is Java's garbage collection?
options: Automatic memory management that reclaims objects with no live references. Generational: Young (Eden + Survivors) for short-lived, Old for long-lived. Collectors: Serial, Parallel, G1 (default), ZGC, Shenandoah
correct_answer: Automatic memory management that reclaims objects with no live references. Generational: Young (Eden + Survivors) for short-lived, Old for long-lived. Collectors: Serial, Parallel, G1 (default), ZGC, Shenandoah

title: What are Java Streams?
options: Declarative API for processing collections; supports filter, map, reduce, collect operations. Lazy evaluation (intermediate ops deferred until terminal op). Can be parallel with .parallelStream(). Not related to I/O streams
correct_answer: Declarative API for processing collections; supports filter, map, reduce, collect operations. Lazy evaluation (intermediate ops deferred until terminal op). Can be parallel with .parallelStream(). Not related to I/O streams

title: What is the difference between abstract class and interface in Java?
description: When to use abstract class vs interface?
options: Abstract class: single inheritance, can have state/constructors/concrete methods. Interface: multiple implementation, only abstract methods (pre-Java 8), default/static methods (Java 8+), private methods (Java 9+). Use interface for contracts, abstract for shared implementation
correct_answer: Abstract class: single inheritance, can have state/constructors/concrete methods. Interface: multiple implementation, only abstract methods (pre-Java 8), default/static methods (Java 8+), private methods (Java 9+). Use interface for contracts, abstract for shared implementation

title: What is Java's Optional class?
options: Optional<T> wraps a possibly-null value; use orElse(), orElseGet(), map(), flatMap(), ifPresent() to handle absence. Intended as return type, not for fields/parameters. Avoids NullPointerException in method chains
correct_answer: Optional<T> wraps a possibly-null value; use orElse(), orElseGet(), map(), flatMap(), ifPresent() to handle absence. Intended as return type, not for fields/parameters. Avoids NullPointerException in method chains

title: What is the difference between HashMap and ConcurrentHashMap?
options: HashMap: not thread-safe, allows one null key, faster for single-threaded. ConcurrentHashMap: thread-safe using segment/bucket locking, no null keys/values, lock-free reads. Use ConcurrentHashMap in multi-threaded code, not Collections.synchronizedMap
correct_answer: HashMap: not thread-safe, allows one null key, faster for single-threaded. ConcurrentHashMap: thread-safe using segment/bucket locking, no null keys/values, lock-free reads. Use ConcurrentHashMap in multi-threaded code, not Collections.synchronizedMap

title: Implement a Java Singleton pattern (thread-safe).
What are Java records?
options: record Point(int x, int y) {} creates an immutable data carrier with auto-generated constructor, getters, equals(), hashCode(), toString(). Concise alternative to POJOs/DTOs. Cannot extend classes but can implement interfaces
correct_answer: record Point(int x, int y) {} creates an immutable data carrier with auto-generated constructor, getters, equals(), hashCode(), toString(). Concise alternative to POJOs/DTOs. Cannot extend classes but can implement interfaces

title: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?
options: Checked exceptions (IOException, SQLException) must be caught or declared with throws; extend Exception. Unchecked exceptions (NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBounds) extend RuntimeException; don't require handling. Errors (OutOfMemoryError) are unrecoverable
correct_answer: Checked exceptions (IOException, SQLException) must be caught or declared with throws; extend Exception. Unchecked exceptions (NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBounds) extend RuntimeException; don't require handling. Errors (OutOfMemoryError) are unrecoverable

title: What is Java's CompletableFuture?
options: Asynchronous programming API; supports chaining (thenApply, thenCompose, thenCombine), error handling (exceptionally, handle), and combining futures (allOf, anyOf). Runs on ForkJoinPool by default or custom executor
correct_answer: Asynchronous programming API; supports chaining (thenApply, thenCompose, thenCombine), error handling (exceptionally, handle), and combining futures (allOf, anyOf). Runs on ForkJoinPool by default or custom executor

title: Implement a Java generic stack.
What are Java sealed classes?
description: How do sealed classes work in Java 17?
options: sealed class Shape permits Circle, Rectangle {} restricts which classes can extend/implement it; subclasses must be final, sealed, or non-sealed. Enables exhaustive pattern matching in switch expressions
correct_answer: sealed class Shape permits Circle, Rectangle {} restricts which classes can extend/implement it; subclasses must be final, sealed, or non-sealed. Enables exhaustive pattern matching in switch expressions

title: What is the difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?
options: String: immutable, safe, creates new object on modification. StringBuilder: mutable, not thread-safe, fastest for single-thread string building. StringBuffer: mutable, thread-safe (synchronized), slower. Use StringBuilder for most concatenation
correct_answer: String: immutable, safe, creates new object on modification. StringBuilder: mutable, not thread-safe, fastest for single-thread string building. StringBuffer: mutable, thread-safe (synchronized), slower. Use StringBuilder for most concatenation

title: Implement a Java producer-consumer with BlockingQueue.
What is Java's virtual threads (Project Loom)?
description: How do virtual threads work in Java 21?
options: Lightweight threads managed by JVM (not OS); millions can run concurrently. Created with Thread.ofVirtual() or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor(). Ideal for I/O-bound tasks; mounted/unmounted from carrier (platform) threads
correct_answer: Lightweight threads managed by JVM (not OS); millions can run concurrently. Created with Thread.ofVirtual() or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor(). Ideal for I/O-bound tasks; mounted/unmounted from carrier (platform) threads

title: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?
description: When to use ArrayList vs LinkedList?
options: ArrayList: O(1) random access, O(n) insert/delete middle, contiguous memory (cache-friendly). LinkedList: O(n) access, O(1) insert/delete at known position, more memory per element. ArrayList is almost always preferred in practice
correct_answer: ArrayList: O(1) random access, O(n) insert/delete middle, contiguous memory (cache-friendly). LinkedList: O(n) access, O(1) insert/delete at known position, more memory per element. ArrayList is almost always preferred in practice

title: Implement the Observer pattern in Java.
What is Java's type erasure?
description: How does type erasure affect generics?
options: At compile time, generic type parameters are replaced with their bounds (or Object if unbounded); e.g., List<String> becomes List at runtime. Cannot use instanceof with generic types, create generic arrays, or get type info at runtime
correct_answer: At compile time, generic type parameters are replaced with their bounds (or Object if unbounded); e.g., List<String> becomes List at runtime. Cannot use instanceof with generic types, create generic arrays, or get type info at runtime

title: Implement a Java LRU cache using LinkedHashMap.
What is the difference between volatile and synchronized?
description: When to use volatile vs synchronized?
options: volatile ensures visibility (reads/writes go to main memory, not CPU cache) but no atomicity for compound actions. synchronized ensures both visibility and atomicity (mutual exclusion). Use volatile for simple flags; synchronized for compound operations
correct_answer: volatile ensures visibility (reads/writes go to main memory, not CPU cache) but no atomicity for compound actions. synchronized ensures both visibility and atomicity (mutual exclusion). Use volatile for simple flags; synchronized for compound operations

title: What are Java annotations and how to create custom ones?
options: @interface defines custom annotations; @Retention sets lifecycle (SOURCE, CLASS, RUNTIME); @Target sets where it applies (TYPE, METHOD, FIELD). Process with reflection (getAnnotation()) or annotation processors at compile time
correct_answer: @interface defines custom annotations; @Retention sets lifecycle (SOURCE, CLASS, RUNTIME); @Target sets where it applies (TYPE, METHOD, FIELD). Process with reflection (getAnnotation()) or annotation processors at compile time

title: Implement a Java functional interface with lambda.
description: Create and use a custom functional interface.

title: What is Java's pattern matching for switch?
description: How does pattern matching work in switch?
options: Java 21 switch supports type patterns: case Integer i -> ... ; case String s when s.length() > 5 -> ... ; with guards, null handling, and exhaustiveness checking. Combines instanceof check and cast in one step
correct_answer: Java 21 switch supports type patterns: case Integer i -> ... ; case String s when s.length() > 5 -> ... ; with guards, null handling, and exhaustiveness checking. Combines instanceof check and cast in one step

title: Implement a Java thread pool executor.
What is dependency injection in Java?
options: Design pattern where dependencies are provided externally (constructor, setter, or field injection) rather than created internally. Enables loose coupling, testability, and swappable implementations. Spring, Guice, and Dagger are popular DI frameworks
correct_answer: Design pattern where dependencies are provided externally (constructor, setter, or field injection) rather than created internally. Enables loose coupling, testability, and swappable implementations. Spring, Guice, and Dagger are popular DI frameworks

title: Implement a Java Builder pattern.
description: Create a Builder for a complex object.

title: What is Java's Stream.collect() with Collectors?
description: How do Collectors work with Streams?
options: Collectors transform stream elements into collections/values: toList(), toSet(), toMap(), groupingBy(), partitioningBy(), joining(), counting(), summarizing. Custom collectors with Collector.of()
correct_answer: Collectors transform stream elements into collections/values: toList(), toSet(), toMap(), groupingBy(), partitioningBy(), joining(), counting(), summarizing. Custom collectors with Collector.of()

title: What is the difference between Comparable and Comparator?
description: When to use Comparable vs Comparator?
options: Comparable: class implements compareTo() for natural ordering (single sort). Comparator: separate class/lambda defining compare() for custom ordering (multiple sorts). Use Comparable for primary order, Comparator for alternate sorts
correct_answer: Comparable: class implements compareTo() for natural ordering (single sort). Comparator: separate class/lambda defining compare() for custom ordering (multiple sorts). Use Comparable for primary order, Comparator for alternate sorts

title: Implement merge sort in Java.
What are Java modules (JPMS)?
description: How does the Java Module System work?
options: module-info.java defines modules with requires (dependencies), exports (public packages), provides/uses (services). Enables strong encapsulation — non-exported packages are inaccessible. Introduced in Java 9 (Project Jigsaw)
correct_answer: module-info.java defines modules with requires (dependencies), exports (public packages), provides/uses (services). Enables strong encapsulation — non-exported packages are inaccessible. Introduced in Java 9 (Project Jigsaw)

title: What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators?
options: Fail-fast iterators (ArrayList, HashMap) throw ConcurrentModificationException if collection is modified during iteration. Fail-safe (CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap) work on a copy/snapshot, allowing concurrent modification
correct_answer: Fail-fast iterators (ArrayList, HashMap) throw ConcurrentModificationException if collection is modified during iteration. Fail-safe (CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap) work on a copy/snapshot, allowing concurrent modification

title: What is Java's try-with-resources?
options: try (Resource r = new Resource()) { } automatically calls r.close() after the block; resource must implement AutoCloseable. Handles suppressed exceptions. Replaces verbose try/finally blocks for resource cleanup
correct_answer: try (Resource r = new Resource()) { } automatically calls r.close() after the block; resource must implement AutoCloseable. Handles suppressed exceptions. Replaces verbose try/finally blocks for resource cleanup

title: Implement a Java concurrent cache with expiry.
description: Create a thread-safe cache with TTL.

title: What is the difference between this() and super() in Java?
description: How do this() and super() work in constructors?
options: this() calls another constructor in the same class (constructor chaining); super() calls the parent class constructor. Both must be the first statement in a constructor; cannot use both in same constructor
correct_answer: this() calls another constructor in the same class (constructor chaining); super() calls the parent class constructor. Both must be the first statement in a constructor; cannot use both in same constructor

title: What is Java's ForkJoinPool?
options: Work-stealing thread pool designed for recursive divide-and-conquer tasks; uses RecursiveTask<V> (returns value) or RecursiveAction (void). Each thread has a deque; idle threads steal from others. Used by parallel streams and CompletableFuture
correct_answer: Work-stealing thread pool designed for recursive divide-and-conquer tasks; uses RecursiveTask<V> (returns value) or RecursiveAction (void). Each thread has a deque; idle threads steal from others. Used by parallel streams and CompletableFuture
